questions.q: Lévy's lemma asserts that a $1$-Lipschitz function $f:S^{n-1}\to\mathbb{R}$ concentrates near its median with what kind of tail?
choices: No tail bound is uniform in $n$
questions.explain: Lévy's lemma gives a Gaussian tail with rate $(n-1)/2$, so the deviation scale is $1/\sqrt n$ — the higher the dimension, the tighter the concentration around the median.

questions.q: If $u,v$ are independent uniform random points on $S^{n-1}$, what is $\mathbb{E}[\langle u,v\rangle^{2}]$?
questions.explain: By symmetry $\mathbb{E}[\langle u,v\rangle^{2}]=\mathbb{E}[u_{1}^{2}]=1/n$. So $\langle u,v\rangle$ is concentrated on a window of width $1/\sqrt n$ around $0$ — generic high-D vectors are almost orthogonal.

questions.q: Why is concentration on the sphere called the "blessing of dimensionality"?
choices: Because volume grows with $n$, giving more room for clusters
Because Lipschitz functions are essentially constant on most of $S^{n-1}$, so empirical averages behave deterministically
Because every set has measure $1$ in the limit $n\to\infty$
Because the curse of dimensionality is a folk myth
questions.explain: High dimension makes Lipschitz functionals (norms, distances, smooth statistics) almost constant on the sphere — predictable behaviour from random inputs is the engine of modern high-D probability.

questions.q: Dvoretzky's theorem says every centred convex body $K\subset\mathbb{R}^{n}$ has an almost-Euclidean section of dimension at least:
questions.explain: Milman's proof gives $k(K,\varepsilon)=\Omega(\varepsilon^{2}\log n)$ for any centred convex body. The $\log n$ scaling is sharp for $\ell^{\infty}$.

questions.q: What does "almost-spherical section" mean for a normed space $(\mathbb{R}^{n},\|\cdot\|)$ on a subspace $E$?
choices: $\|\cdot\|$ restricted to $E$ is exactly the Euclidean norm
$E$ is contained in the unit sphere of $\|\cdot\|$
There is $c>0$ with $c\,|x|\le\|x\|\le (1+\varepsilon)c\,|x|$ for all $x\in E$
questions.explain: An $(1+\varepsilon)$-Euclidean section is a subspace on which the given norm is sandwiched between two Hilbert-space norms with ratio $1+\varepsilon$ — the geometry on $E$ looks Euclidean up to distortion $\varepsilon$.

questions.q: Which proof technique drives Dvoretzky's theorem?
choices: The Hahn–Banach theorem applied to the polar body
Concentration of $\|x\|$ on the sphere combined with an $\varepsilon$-net argument on Grassmannians
Spectral decomposition of the moment-of-inertia tensor
Riemann–Roch on a curve of high genus
questions.explain: Milman's argument: Lévy concentration shows $\|\cdot\|$ is essentially constant on most of $S^{n-1}$; an $\varepsilon$-net on the Grassmannian then produces a subspace where the deviation is small uniformly.

questions.q: How does the JL target dimension $k$ scale with the number of points $N$ and distortion $\varepsilon$?
questions.explain: The JL lemma gives $k=O(\varepsilon^{-2}\log N)$ — independent of the ambient $n$. The $\varepsilon^{-2}$ comes from the variance of the projected length; the $\log N$ from a union bound over $\binom{N}{2}$ pairs.

questions.q: For a random $k\times n$ Gaussian matrix $G$ with i.i.d.\ $\mathcal{N}(0,1/k)$ entries and a fixed unit vector $x$, what is $\mathbb{E}[\|Gx\|^{2}]$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{E}[\|Gx\|^{2}]=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathbb{E}[(g_{i}\cdot x)^{2}]=k\cdot(1/k)\|x\|^{2}=\|x\|^{2}=1$. The JL embedding is unbiased; concentration around $1$ is the content of the lemma.

questions.q: Why does JL preserve pairwise distances, not arbitrary geometric features?
choices: It uses only differences $x_{i}-x_{j}$, applies the lemma to each, and union-bounds
It assumes the data lies on a low-dimensional manifold
The Gaussian projection preserves all rank-1 matrices
questions.explain: JL lifts a one-vector concentration ($\|Gx\|\approx\|x\|$ with high probability) to all $\binom{N}{2}$ difference vectors via a union bound — giving multiplicative distance preservation but not, say, angle preservation against arbitrary subspaces.

questions.q: Talagrand's convex-distance inequality is a concentration statement on which kind of probability space?
choices: Compact Riemannian manifolds with positive curvature
questions.explain: Talagrand's setting is product probability spaces. Its strength is that the bound depends on the convex distance, not on a metric Lipschitz constant — so it controls many functionals that aren't Lipschitz on the Hamming cube.

questions.q: For a convex $1$-Lipschitz $f:[0,1]^{n}\to\mathbb{R}$ with $X$ uniform on $[0,1]^{n}$, Talagrand gives a tail of order:
questions.explain: Talagrand's bound is dimension-free in $n$: $\mathbb{P}(|f-\mathrm{med}\,f|>t)\le 4e^{-t^{2}/4}$. The Lipschitz constant absorbs scaling; one extra slot does not increase the deviation.

questions.q: Why is convexity needed in Talagrand's statement (it fails for general $1$-Lipschitz $f$ on the cube)?
choices: Convexity is just a convenience; the inequality holds for any Lipschitz $f$
Convexity lets the convex distance dominate the increment, so the certificate-based induction closes
questions.explain: Talagrand's induction over coordinates uses convexity to control how a coordinate-flip changes $f$ in terms of the convex distance — giving the dimension-free constant. For non-convex Lipschitz $f$ on the Hamming cube the standard bound has a $\sqrt n$ in the deviation.

questions.q: Among Borel sets $A\subset\mathbb{R}^{n}$ of fixed Gaussian measure, which extremise the Gaussian surface area?
choices: Cones with vertex at the origin
questions.explain: Borell–Sudakov–Tsirelson: halfspaces are the Gaussian-isoperimetric minimisers, in contrast with Euclidean isoperimetry where balls are optimal.

questions.q: What is the Poincaré–Maxwell limit linking the sphere and the Gaussian?
choices: Uniform measure on $S^{n-1}(\sqrt n)$ projected onto the first $k$ coordinates converges to $\mathcal{N}(0,I_{k})$ as $n\to\infty$
$S^{n-1}$ has constant Gaussian curvature, equal to that of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$
questions.explain: Project the uniform measure on the radius-$\sqrt n$ sphere onto $k$ fixed coordinates: the marginal converges to standard $k$-dim Gaussian. This is why concentration on $S^{n-1}$ "becomes" Gaussian concentration in the limit.

questions.q: Which functional inequality, due to Gross, is equivalent to Gaussian isoperimetry up to dimension-free constants?
questions.explain: Gross's log-Sobolev inequality for the Gaussian measure $\gamma$ controls entropy by Dirichlet energy and is dimension-free. Bobkov's functional refinement is the sharp form that recovers Gaussian isoperimetry directly.

questions.q: Compressed sensing recovers a $k$-sparse signal $x\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$ from $m$ random linear measurements when:
choices: $m\ge ck\log(n/k)$, where the sensing matrix satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property
questions.explain: Candès–Tao show $\ell^{1}$-minimisation recovers $k$-sparse $x$ exactly from $m=O(k\log(n/k))$ Gaussian measurements. RIP is proved via JL-type concentration on every $k$-dim coordinate subspace.

questions.q: What is the limiting empirical spectral distribution of $\frac{1}{n}WW^{*}$ where $W$ is $m\times n$ with i.i.d.\ entries of mean $0$ and variance $1$, as $m,n\to\infty$ with $m/n\to\lambda$?
questions.explain: The Wishart-type matrix has empirical spectrum converging to the Marchenko–Pastur distribution with parameter $\lambda$. The semicircle is for symmetric Wigner matrices; Tracy–Widom is for the largest eigenvalue.

questions.q: VC-style PAC bounds give a sample complexity of order $m=O(d/\varepsilon^{2})$ for a hypothesis class of VC dimension $d$. The bound rests on which probabilistic tool?
choices: Uniform concentration of empirical means (a Talagrand / chaining argument over the class)
The spectral theorem for self-adjoint operators
questions.explain: PAC learning bounds use uniform concentration: empirical means concentrate around expectations uniformly over the hypothesis class. Talagrand-type inequalities and chaining are the modern tools.
